title: Quick check: four causes of an offline agent

questions.prompt: Which set names all four causes of an offline agent?
choices: Powered off; service stopped; network blocked; uninstalled.

choices.label: Powered off; AV interference; policy block; unsupported OS.
choices.rationale: Mixes offline causes with install-failure causes from lessons 14-16.

choices.label: Crashed; rebooted; uninstalled; replaced.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's enumeration.

choices.label: Online; offline; pending; archived.
choices.rationale: Portal states, not offline causes.

questions.explanation: Four causes hiding behind one portal status.

questions.prompt: The endpoint shows online in the RMM, but Huntress shows offline. The Huntress service is stopped on the endpoint. Which cause is this?

choices.label: Powered off.
choices.rationale: The endpoint is online in the RMM, so it isn't powered off.

choices.label: Network blocked.
choices.rationale: Network-blocked has the service running and the network call failing.

choices.label: Uninstalled.
choices.rationale: Uninstalled means the service entry doesn't exist at all; here it exists and is stopped.

questions.explanation: RMM and Huntress disagreeing about the same endpoint is the canonical service-stopped fingerprint.

questions.prompt: You diagnose an offline agent as 'uninstalled.' What should you do BEFORE re-pushing?
choices: Investigate why it was uninstalled (deliberate user/admin removal, deployment-tool cleanup, AV update removing it as a perceived threat, sysprep wipe). If AV removed it, re-install hits the same condition.

choices.label: Just re-push the install; that's what uninstalled means.
choices.rationale: Shotgun fix. Hits the same failure if the cause is still present.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket asking them to re-add the agent server-side.
choices.rationale: Misuses support and isn't a real workflow.

choices.label: Wait 24 hours to see if the agent comes back on its own.
choices.rationale: Uninstalled agents don't come back without a re-push.

questions.explanation: Cause-investigation before re-push is the named discipline.

questions.prompt: Three offlines have been there for more than 24 hours: a reception workstation, a production server, an executive laptop. The customer wants them sorted. Where do you start?

choices.label: Re-push the install to all three.
choices.rationale: Three different endpoints, three potentially different causes, one indiscriminate fix.

choices: Run the two-question diagnostic on each (RMM online? Huntress service running?). Categorise before fixing.

choices.label: Reboot all three from the RMM.
choices.rationale: Reboots fix some service-stopped cases by accident; don't help with the other three causes. Heavyweight on a production server.

questions.explanation: Two minutes of categorisation saves an hour of misapplied fixes.

questions.prompt: Results: receptionist's workstation powered off (away two weeks). Production server online in RMM, Huntress service running, agent log shows phone-home failures. Executive laptop online in RMM, Huntress service entry missing. What's the action set?
choices: Leave the receptionist's workstation (powered off; no action). Treat the production server as network-blocked per lesson 17. Investigate why the executive laptop is uninstalled before re-pushing.

choices.label: Re-push to the executive laptop immediately because at least one of them needs the install.
choices.rationale: You'd want to know why the agent was removed first. AV-removed-it or user-removed-it is plausible on an executive laptop.

choices.label: Escalate to the senior because three offlines is a lot.
choices.rationale: Three offlines on a 200-endpoint customer is well within normal range, and you've categorised them.

questions.explanation: Each cause gets the right diagnostic path; investigate-then-re-push catches AV-removed-it or user-removed-it cases.

questions.prompt: Why is 'offline means the agent is broken' called out as a misconception?
choices: Most offlines are powered off. Don't reach for a fix before confirming the endpoint is actually up.

choices.label: Offline is a rare state that rarely happens in practice.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson. Offlines happen routinely.

choices.label: Only Huntress support can identify a broken agent.
choices.rationale: Misuses support. The diagnostic is local.

choices.label: Offline always means uninstalled.
choices.rationale: Conflates two different causes.

questions.explanation: Single most common cause and the cheapest to recognise.

questions.prompt: 'Service-stopped agents can be fixed by re-installing.' Why is this a misconception?
choices: Sometimes it works, but re-installing without knowing why the service stopped is shotgun debugging. The service stopped for a reason; find it.

choices.label: Re-installing is impossible on a service-stopped state.
choices.rationale: The install would run; the underlying cause might still stop the service.

choices.label: Service-stopped agents resolve themselves within 24 hours.
choices.rationale: Not a real behaviour.

choices.label: Re-installing voids the warranty.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

questions.explanation: Same family as the uninstalled-re-push-without-cause anti-pattern.

questions.prompt: A whole site shows offline at once. What's the likely cause?
choices: Network blocked at the site level (firewall change, ISP outage) until proven otherwise.

choices.label: All endpoints uninstalled simultaneously.
choices.rationale: Coincidence at scale is extremely unlikely.

choices.label: All endpoints powered off simultaneously.
choices.rationale: Possible only if there's a known site-wide outage; otherwise network-level is more likely.

choices.label: All endpoints have crashed services.
choices.rationale: Unlikely without a shared cause.

questions.explanation: Mass simultaneous offline is the canonical network-level fingerprint.

questions.prompt: Once you've categorised an offline as 'network blocked,' which lesson holds the diagnostic?

choices.label: The AV-conflict lesson (14).
choices.rationale: AV interference is a different failure class.

choices.label: The clean-uninstall lesson (21).
choices.rationale: Uninstall/decommission isn't a diagnostic for blocked networks.

choices.label: The coverage-verification lesson.
choices.rationale: Coverage verification isn't the network-blocked diagnostic.

questions.explanation: Network-blocked routes to the same diagnostic as a blocked-outbound install failure.

questions.prompt: Order the two-question diagnostic for separating the four causes.
items: Question 1: Is the endpoint online in the RMM?
If RMM says No, the cause is Powered off. No action needed.
If RMM says Yes, ask Question 2: Is the Huntress service running on the endpoint?
If the service is missing entirely, the cause is Uninstalled.
If the service exists but is stopped, the cause is Service stopped.
If the service is running, the cause is Network blocked.
questions.explanation: Two questions sort all four causes in under a minute.
